summary:
The “needle” causing the dramatic performance cliff was the hashCode implementation in Product.java:

Old code  
```
@Override
public int hashCode() {
    return category.hashCode();   // only 10 distinct values -> huge collisions
}
```

Because equals() compares Products solely by SKU, the hashCode must also be based on SKU; otherwise every lookup falls into one of ~10 buckets, producing O(n) chains once the data grow.

Fix applied  
```
@Override
public int hashCode() {
    // Use the unique key (SKU) to spread entries evenly
    return sku.hashCode();
}
```

After recompiling, all tests pass:

```
=== Performance Cliff Hash Map Test ===
--- Small dataset (100 items) ---
Average lookup: 30.02 us
--- Hash distribution analysis ---
Non-empty buckets: 5991 (36.6%)
Max chain length: 4
PASS: Hash map distributes items evenly across buckets
```

Lookup chains stay short, eliminating the performance cliff.
